I'm a director at a chemical company. We specialize in specialty chemicals.
Our products are tested before they are shipped, and all regulations are followed.
It's a fairly complex process, but basically we make what other factories can't make safely.
We work closely with distributors in Europe and Asia.
Good morning. I saw your sign -- specialty chemicals, is that right? So, what exactly does your company do?
We specialize in specialty chemicals. Our products are manufactured in Guarulhos, near São Paulo, and I run production and regulatory affairs.
Impressive. We distribute across France and Belgium, so quality is everything for us. How do you make sure your products meet a high standard?
Every batch is tested before it is released, and the whole manufacturing process is monitored by our quality control team.
And your supply chain? In our market, nothing is sold without proper certification. Where are your raw materials sourced?
Most of our raw materials are sourced from trusted vendors in Asia and Europe, and each shipment is checked when it arrives. The plant is certified to international standards.
That is exactly what we look for. Regulatory compliance is not optional for us. Could we talk about becoming your distributor for the region?
I'd be glad to. Let me send you our company profile and our compliance documents -- and the first shipment could be arranged for early next year.
We specialize in specialty chemicals, and I run production and regulatory affairs.
Our products are tested before they are shipped.
Our raw materials are sourced from vendors in Asia and Europe.
All regulations are set by the health authority, and every one is followed.
It's a fairly complex process, but basically we make the raw materials others can't make safely.

Good afternoon, Ms. Ito, this is Daniel Lefebvre. We met at your booth at the Lyon fair last week, and I wanted to follow up. I was very impressed by what I saw. My company distributes specialty chemicals across France and Belgium, and we are always looking for new partners whose products are made to a high standard. What caught my attention was one thing you said: that every batch is tested before it is released, and that your plant is certified to international standards. That matters to us, because in our market a product without proper certification simply cannot be sold. So here is what I would like to propose. If your raw materials are sourced responsibly and your supply chain can reach Europe, I believe we could become your distributor for the region. Nothing is decided yet, of course. But the first shipment could be arranged for early next year, once the paperwork is reviewed on both sides. Please send me your company profile and your compliance documents when you have a moment. You explained everything so clearly at the fair, in English, and that is exactly the kind of partner we want. I look forward to hearing from you. Thank you, Ms. Ito.

Welcome back to The Global Table, the show for people who do business across borders. Today, one skill that is worth more than perfect grammar: explaining what you do without getting lost. Here is the trap. You are an expert. You know your industry inside out. So when someone at a dinner asks, what do you do, you either say three boring words, I work in chemicals, or you drown them in detail they did not ask for. Neither one works. The people who do this well follow a simple pattern, and you can steal it tonight. First, one sentence for the field: our company specializes in specialty chemicals. Second, one sentence for the value, and this is where the passive voice becomes your best friend, because in business we care about the process, not the person. Our products are tested before they are shipped. Regulations are set by the health authority, and we follow every one of them. Notice how you never have to say who tests, or who sets the rules. The process is the star. Third, and only if they seem interested, one human detail: it is a fairly complex process, but basically we make the raw materials that other factories cannot make safely themselves. That is it. Field, value, human detail. Three sentences, and you sound like someone who knows exactly what they do, because you do. The goal was never to say everything. The goal is to be understood.
